text.body_text: How much do you make? Have you ever contemplated suicide? Are you now or have you ever been...? Are you aware of the fact...? I have here before me.... Electrical information devices for universal, tyrannical womb-to-tomb surveillance are causing a very serious dilemma between our claim to privacy and the community's need to know. The older, traditional ideas of private, isolated thoughts and actions—the patterns of mechanistic technologies—are very seriously threatened by new methods of instantaneous electric information retrieval, by the electrically computerized dossier bank—that one big gossip column that is unforgiving, unforgetful and from which there is no redemption, no erasure of early "mistakes." We have already reached a point where remedial control, born out of knowledge of media and their total effects on all of us, must be exerted. How shall the new environment be programmed now that we have become so involved with each other, now that all of us have become the unwitting work force for social change? What's that buzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzing?

images.subject: A fingerprint — a single, detailed thumbprint or index fingerprint rendered in black ink.
images.composition: Small square image in the upper-left area of the left page, tucked next to the body text. High contrast black on white.
images.relationship_to_text: Literalizes the theme of individual identity under surveillance. The fingerprint is the most basic biometric identifier — the thing that makes 'you' uniquely trackable. Placed right next to text about dossier banks and surveillance, it concretizes the abstract threat.

images.subject: Concentric black and white circles forming a target or bullseye pattern. The circles radiate outward from a white center, filling the entire right page.
images.composition: Full-page graphic. Perfect concentric circles, high contrast black and white. Hypnotic, Op Art quality. The center is slightly left of the page center, creating a sense of the pattern extending beyond the page edge.
images.relationship_to_text: The target/bullseye directly enacts the argument about surveillance — 'you' are the target. The Op Art quality also creates a sense of being watched, of the eye being drawn in. The concentric circles suggest the expanding reach of surveillance systems, radiating outward from the individual at the center.

design.layout_description: Left page: large bold 'you' at top, fingerprint inset upper-left beside body text, body text in a single column occupying most of the page. Right page: full-bleed concentric circle target graphic. Strong asymmetry — text-heavy left, image-only right.

typography.body_font_style: Serif, medium weight, justified, single column approximately 50% of page width. Standard body text size.
typography.display_font_style: Sans-serif, bold, very large — 'you' occupies roughly 1/6 of the left page height. Lowercase. Aggressive, direct.
special_treatments: The word 'buzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzzing' stretches out with repeated z's — typographic onomatopoeia mimicking an electronic buzz/drone.

design.color_and_tone: High contrast throughout. The right page is especially stark — pure black and white with no gray tones.
design.left_right_relationship: Call-and-response. The left page asks the question ('you' — who are you? what do they know about you?), and the right page answers with the target: you are being aimed at. The fingerprint on the left (identity) maps to the bullseye on the right (target of surveillance).

rhetoric.argument: Electronic information technology has created a universal surveillance apparatus that threatens individual privacy. The 'computerized dossier bank' is an unforgiving, permanent record. We have moved from a world of private thoughts to one of total exposure, and we are all complicit in this transformation.
rhetoric.rhetorical_strategy: Confrontation. The spread opens with aggressive interrogation questions ('How much do you make? Have you ever contemplated suicide?') that mimic the invasive questioning of surveillance systems. The pronoun 'you' — as both display text and repeated in the body — makes the reader the subject of surveillance, not a passive observer of it.
rhetoric.design_argument_description: The spread performs surveillance on the reader. The giant 'you' singles them out. The fingerprint identifies them. The target on the facing page aims at them. The reader cannot passively read about surveillance — they experience being its object. The buzzing final word mimics the electronic hum of the surveillance apparatus itself.
rhetoric.reader_experience: Confrontation and unease. The reader feels personally addressed, exposed, targeted. The shift from abstract argument to 'you' makes the threat intimate.

themes.mapping_rationale: McLuhan's 'computerized dossier bank' maps directly to today's algorithmic profiling, data brokerage, and AI-driven surveillance systems. The 'womb-to-tomb surveillance' he describes in 1967 has been realized in ways he could not have imagined — browser histories, location tracking, facial recognition, social media graphs. The 'unforgiving, unforgetful' quality of the dossier bank is today's internet permanence. The buzzing at the end anticipates the ambient, always-on quality of digital surveillance.

progression.relationship_to_previous: Continues the early section's pattern of establishing McLuhan's core concerns. Moves from the more abstract opening spreads into a concrete argument about surveillance.
progression.relationship_to_next: Sets up further exploration of how electric technology changes the relationship between individual and collective.

spreads.notes: The interrogation-style opening ('How much do you make? Have you ever contemplated suicide?') echoes the language of McCarthyism ('Are you now or have you ever been...') — connecting electronic surveillance to political persecution. This historical resonance would be worth noting in the contemporary version, where algorithmic systems have their own forms of political profiling.
